pattern: The cost of forcing any one substring into the cheapest semi-palindrome shape can be worked out on its own, so splitting the whole string into k pieces optimally becomes an ordinary partition dynamic program layered on top of those precomputed per-substring costs.
hints: For a single substring, checking whether a candidate divisor d makes it a semi-palindrome only requires grouping its characters by position modulo d and counting mismatches within each group as if making that group a palindrome on its own -- try every valid divisor and keep the cheapest.
Precompute that cheapest cost for every possible contiguous substring of s once, since the same substring cost may be needed for several different ways of splitting the string.

With those substring costs available, run a partition dynamic program: dp[end][pieces] = the minimum total cost of splitting the first `end` characters into exactly `pieces` semi-palindromic substrings, built by extending from every earlier cut point.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one part already qualifies and the other needs one change
statement example, both halves need exactly one change each
statement example, every part is already a semi-palindrome
shortest possible string with the only allowed split
k at its maximum relative to the string length
a single part covering the whole string
a string that is already fully palindromic but split forces extra changes
repeating three-letter pattern that is a semi-palindrome only with divisor three

stress.description: moderate-length strings over a small alphabet with a modest k, exercising the partition DP
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(4, min(size, 60))
    if n % 2 == 1:
        n -= 1
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('abc') for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, max(1, n // 2))
    return [s, k]

complexity.time: O(n^2 * k) after O(n^3) substring precompute
